answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research on this, and the broad pattern is pretty consistent:

**Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work tends to predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than salary level does**, especially once pay is already “good enough” rather than financially constraining.

A few research-backed points that are especially relevant to your situation:

## 1) Job content matters a lot

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” usually shows up as things like:

- **skill variety**
- **challenge / learning**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback / sense of progress**

These are among the strongest predictors of **job satisfaction**, **intrinsic motivation**, and lower **turnover intention**.

### Classic line of research
- **Hackman & Oldham**’s job characteristics model argued that jobs high in variety, autonomy, significance, etc. produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction.
- Later **meta-analyses** supported this pretty strongly.

### Good meta-analytic summary
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a large work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback** were **moderately to strongly associated** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal work motivation
  - organizational commitment
  - lower burnout / withdrawal

So if one job is genuinely much more intellectually engaging, that is not a trivial preference; it is one of the things research says matters most.

---

## 2) “Meaningful work” is strongly linked to happiness and satisfaction

A newer literature looks directly at **meaningful work** rather than just job design.

### Main result
Meta-analyses find that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- **higher job satisfaction**
- **higher work engagement**
- **higher life satisfaction / general well-being**
- **lower burnout**
- **lower intention to quit**

One commonly cited review is:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** on outcomes of meaningful work.

I’d summarize that literature this way: **meaningful/interesting work is not just a “nice-to-have”; it is reliably associated with better psychological outcomes.**

---

## 3) Pay matters — but usually less than people expect

There is also good research on the relationship between **pay** and **job satisfaction**.

### Important result
- **Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analysis found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

This is one of the most useful findings for decisions like yours. It does **not** mean money doesn’t matter. It does mean:

- once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is low,
- a somewhat higher salary often has a **smaller effect** on happiness than people intuitively think.

Also, research suggests that **pay fairness / relative pay** often matters more than absolute pay level.

So a **bit higher salary** is real, but on average it usually does **not** buy as much satisfaction as better day-to-day work content.

---

## 4) For knowledge work, intrinsic motivation is especially important

For research/engineering/creative work, this may matter even more.

### Relevant research
- **Amabile’s work** on creativity and knowledge workers emphasizes that **intrinsic motivation** and a sense of making progress on meaningful work are major drivers of positive affect and performance.
- In knowledge-intensive work, daily experience — “is this work engaging?” — strongly shapes motivation and energy.

That is especially relevant for a research engineer. In more routine jobs, compensation or title may play a bigger relative role. In highly creative/technical work, **interest in the actual problems** tends to matter a lot.

---

## 5) Leadership opportunities can help — but only if they fit you

You also mentioned something important: **leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted**.

Research on **person-job fit** and **need satisfaction** says this can matter a lot too.

### Two relevant literatures

#### a) Person-job / person-organization fit
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis found that better fit is associated with:
  - higher job satisfaction
  - higher commitment
  - lower intention to leave

If “leading people” is a real aspiration, that’s not just extrinsic status — it can be part of fit.

#### b) Self-determination theory
Research on **autonomy, competence, and relatedness** consistently finds that when work satisfies these needs, people do better psychologically.

- Leadership can increase:
  - autonomy
  - impact
  - sense of competence
  - relatedness / contribution

But it can also reduce:
  - time spent on the technical work you actually enjoy
  - deep work / flow
  - perceived mastery if management is less aligned with your strengths

So from a research perspective, the key question is not “management = better or worse?” but **“which role better satisfies your underlying motives?”**

If leading a team is deeply important to you, that can be a genuine source of meaning and satisfaction.  
If it mainly sounds attractive in the abstract, but the work itself is much less interesting, then the daily experience may still be worse.

---

## 6) What about regret specifically?

This is the part where the research is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong body of studies asking exactly:

> “Do people later regret staying in the more interesting job rather than taking the better-paid/managerial one?”

There is more evidence on **satisfaction and well-being** than on explicit **regret**.

That said, a few adjacent findings are relevant:

### a) People adapt to extrinsic gains
Promotions, raises, and status increases often produce a positive effect, but part of that effect tends to **fade over time**.

### b) Daily experience keeps recurring
Interesting work, autonomy, and meaning affect you **every workday**, not just once. That makes them especially important for ongoing well-being.

### c) Promotions are mixed
Panel studies on promotions generally show some combination of:
- increased satisfaction from status/pay/opportunity
- increased stress, workload, and pressure
- benefits that are sometimes **temporary**

So if the Anthropic role gives you leadership but much less interesting work, research would predict something like:

- **the leadership aspect may be satisfying if it matches your identity**
- **the salary bump alone is unlikely to compensate much**
- **the loss of day-to-day interest could matter quite a lot**

But I’d say this as a probabilistic research summary, not a certainty.

---

## 7) If I compress the evidence into one sentence

If both jobs already pay very well, then **day-to-day job quality** — especially **interest, meaning, autonomy, and fit** — is usually a stronger predictor of long-run satisfaction than a modest salary increase.

---

## 8) A research-based way to frame your choice

Instead of “Meta vs Anthropic,” the literature suggests comparing these dimensions:

1. **Interestingness / meaningfulness of the actual work**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Learning / skill growth**
4. **Fit with your long-term identity**  
   - researcher?
   - manager/leader?
   - builder of teams?
5. **Quality of daily experience**
6. **Compensation fairness / financial impact**
7. **Stress and workload**
8. **Future option value**

That is much closer to how the well-being literature would analyze it than simply “which has higher salary/title?”

---

## 9) Bottom line from the research

### What the evidence supports fairly strongly
- People in more interesting / meaningful / autonomous jobs are **usually more satisfied** with their jobs.
- Those factors also spill over somewhat into **overall well-being**.
- **Pay matters, but less strongly** than most people assume once you’re already highly paid.
- **Fit** matters a lot: if leading a team is a core aspiration, that may compensate for some loss of work-interest — but not necessarily all.

### What the evidence does *not* strongly establish
- A clean universal answer to “you will regret choosing X.”
- A precise tradeoff like “interesting work is worth exactly Y% salary.”
- A single conclusion independent of your motives.

---

## Useful references to look up

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976)** — work design / job characteristics
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work
- **Van den Broeck, A., Ferris, D. L., Chang, C.-H., & Rosen, C. C. (2016)** — self-determination needs at work
- **Amabile, T. M., & Kramer, S. J.** — progress/meaning in knowledge work

If you want, I can do one of two things next:

1. give you a **shorter “executive summary”** of the literature, or  
2. turn this into a **decision matrix based only on research-backed factors** for your exact Meta vs Anthropic choice.

Promotions can increase job satisfaction, influenced by perceived status and future prospects, but they also bring additional demands and stress, making overall well-being less predictably positive. Additionally, job satisfaction tends to enhance overall life satisfaction. **Evaluating regret and motivation**

It seems that research doesn't definitively indicate which type of regret will dominate in the user's case. For those involved in research or creative work, intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of creativity and performance quality. I should note that less engaging work not only diminishes enjoyment but may also impact excellence. **Analyzing job characteristics**

I'm looking at Man & Oldham's core job characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. It’s interesting how skill variety and significance relate to internal motivation and job satisfaction. If I were to make predictions based on the literature, one takeaway could be that modest salary increases in satisfying roles often lead to better well-being for those with more interest and autonomy. However, if the new position offers substantial autonomy or aligns with leadership goals, this could change things. I’ll need to compile references, aiming for around eight.
**Evaluating research on job design**

I'm considering the support from longitudinal and meta-analytic studies that suggest a causal effect of job design on well-being. It might be valuable to mention how job crafting can increase meaning and autonomy, thereby improving overall satisfaction. I want to verify some key studies: Judge et al. found a correlation of around 0.15, while Humphrey noted a strong connection between work design and satisfaction. answer:
Yes—there’s a fair amount of research relevant to this, though not much that directly studies your exact tradeoff (“more interesting IC research role” vs “less interesting role but more pay + first people leadership”).

The broad takeaway from the literature is:

**Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is usually a stronger predictor of long-term job satisfaction than salary differences, once pay is already good/fair.**  
But **leadership opportunity can also increase satisfaction** if it fulfills an important personal goal, increases autonomy, or gives a stronger sense of impact.

## What the research most consistently finds

### 1) Intrinsic qualities of work matter a lot
A large body of organizational psychology research finds that people are happier in jobs with:

- **autonomy/control**
- **skill variety / intellectually engaging work**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback / sense of progress**
- **good fit between the person and the role**

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses have supported the basic idea.

A particularly important meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of work design research.  
  They found that features like **autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and social support** are strongly associated with **job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, engagement, and lower strain**.

In plain English:  
If a job is genuinely more interesting, gives more learning, and feels more important, that usually predicts higher day-to-day work satisfaction.

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly tied to well-being
There is also a more recent literature on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis  
  Meaningful work is associated with **higher job satisfaction, life satisfaction, work engagement, and commitment**, and with **lower burnout and turnover intentions**.

This is relevant because “interesting” work often overlaps with:
- intellectual curiosity
- feeling that the work matters
- feeling aligned with your values/identity

Those are not trivial “nice-to-haves”; they are among the most reliable predictors of liking your job.

### 3) Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors
A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Found that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** have strong relationships with **job satisfaction**, **commitment**, and **intent to quit**.

If “Meta research engineer doing very interesting stuff” is a stronger fit to your interests and identity than “Anthropic leading a small team doing less interesting work,” that fit difference is likely psychologically important.

---

## How does this compare with money?

### 4) Pay matters, but usually less than people think
There is a strong tendency for people to overestimate how much a salary bump will improve their ongoing work happiness.

A key meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

The exact correlations depend on how pay is measured, but the broad conclusion is stable:  
**money helps, but it explains much less of job satisfaction than many non-monetary job features do.**

This does **not** mean salary is irrelevant. It matters a lot when:
- current compensation feels unfair
- the increase meaningfully changes lifestyle/security
- compensation signals status or future trajectory

But when someone is already in a highly paid role, the literature usually finds that differences in **interestingness, autonomy, meaning, and fit** have more impact on everyday satisfaction than a “somewhat higher” salary.

---

## What about leadership opportunity?

### 5) Promotions and advancement usually help satisfaction — but not always
There is research showing that promotions tend to increase job satisfaction on average:

- **Kosteas (2011)**, *Industrial Relations*  
  Promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction, especially because they affect not only status but also expectations about future growth.

So “leading a small team” is not a trivial factor. It can improve satisfaction through:
- autonomy
- status
- influence
- developmental growth
- alignment with a long-held career goal

But the literature also suggests the effect is **heterogeneous**:
- more responsibility can mean more stress
- management often reduces time spent on the technical/intellectual parts of work
- satisfaction depends heavily on whether the person actually wants people management

In other words:  
**leadership opportunity is a real positive factor in the research, but it does not automatically beat interesting work.**  
Its value depends a lot on whether leading people is central to your identity and aspirations.

---

## Does job satisfaction spill over into overall happiness?

### 6) Yes, moderately
This matters because you asked about being “happier,” not just “more satisfied at work.”

A long-standing finding is that **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately related**.

- **Tait, Padgett, & Baldwin (1989)**, meta-analysis  
  Found a substantial positive relationship between **job satisfaction** and **life satisfaction**.

So if one role is likely to produce consistently better work experience, that often does affect overall well-being, not just your mood from 9 to 5.

---

## What about regret?

### 7) The direct evidence on career regret is weaker
There is **much less direct research** on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/leadership one?” specifically.

What exists is more general regret psychology:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**  
  Over the long run, people tend to regret **inactions** more than **actions**.

That finding is often invoked in career advice, but it’s too general to resolve your case. It could cut either way:

- If **leading a team** is a deep, long-held aspiration, declining it could become an “inaction regret.”
- If **doing genuinely fascinating work** is central to who you are, leaving it for a less interesting role could also become a regret—especially if you later feel you traded daily intellectual engagement for marginal pay/status.

So on regret specifically, the research is **not strong enough to give a clean answer**. The strongest evidence is on **current and sustained job satisfaction**, not retrospective regret.

---

## One especially relevant point for research/creative work

For technical research roles, intrinsic motivation may matter even more than in many other jobs.

- **Amabile’s creativity research** and later work on intrinsic motivation
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**, meta-analysis

These lines of work suggest that for **creative, complex, exploratory work**, **intrinsic motivation** is especially important for persistence, creativity, and quality of output.

So in a role like research engineering, “interestingness” is not only about enjoyment; it is often tied to:
- better performance
- more sustained energy
- more learning
- less burnout from motivational mismatch

That makes the cost of moving to “much less interesting” work potentially larger than it would be in a more routine job.

---

## If I translate the literature into your exact tradeoff

A research-based summary would be:

### The literature would generally predict:
- If both jobs are already very well-paid,
- and the salary difference is only moderate,
- and the Meta role is **substantially more interesting**,

then **staying in the more interesting role is more likely to maximize day-to-day job satisfaction and probably overall well-being**.

### But that prediction gets weaker if:
- leading a team is a major personal goal for you,
- the new role increases autonomy and perceived impact,
- or the current role is blocking an important kind of growth you care about.

In that case, the literature on **fit, advancement, and goal attainment** would say the leadership opportunity could outweigh lower interestingness.

So the research does **not** say “always choose the more interesting job.”  
It says:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction.**
2. **Money has smaller effects than many people assume once compensation is already high enough.**
3. **Leadership opportunity matters, especially when it fulfills a valued aspiration.**
4. **There is little direct evidence that people systematically regret choosing interesting work; the stronger evidence is about ongoing satisfaction, where interesting work usually wins unless another factor is deeply identity-relevant.**

---

## Most relevant papers to look up

If you want the shortest reading list, I’d start with these:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Journal of Applied Psychology.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Journal of Vocational Behavior.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   Personnel Psychology.

4. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*  

5. **Kosteas (2011)**  
   *Job satisfaction and promotions.*  
   Industrial Relations.

6. **Tait, Padgett, & Baldwin (1989)**  
   *Job and life satisfaction: A reevaluation of the strength of the relationship and gender effects as a function of the date of the study.*

7. **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**  
   *Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis.*  
   Psychological Bulletin.

8. **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**  
   *The experience of regret: What, when, and why.*  
   Psychological Review.

If you want, I can do a second pass that is even more practical:  
I can turn this literature into a **decision matrix** specifically for your case, with factors like interestingness, leadership aspiration, future option value, burnout risk, and predicted regret—still grounded in research rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research adjacent to this, though not usually framed exactly as “should I keep the more interesting job or take the somewhat higher-paying less-interesting one?”

The short version from the literature is:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
- **Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect**, especially once pay is already high/adequate.
- **Promotions or leadership roles often increase satisfaction in the short run**, but the long-run effect is mixed and depends a lot on whether the person actually wants the new kind of work.
- **There is much less direct research on “regret”**, but there is a lot of research showing that people often **overweight salary/status** and **underweight day-to-day experience** when making choices.

## 1) “Interesting work” really does matter a lot

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under related constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** such as autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback
- **person-job fit**

These are all strongly associated with higher wellbeing at work.

### Strong evidence: work design
A major meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

They integrated a large literature on work design and found that characteristics like **autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback** were **moderately to strongly related** to:
- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout / strain

That’s important because “interesting work” often means exactly those features.

### Strong evidence: meaningful work
Another useful one:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on **meaningful work**

Their result: people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report substantially higher:
- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life satisfaction**

and lower:
- **distress**
- **turnover intentions**

The associations here are generally **much larger than the typical pay–satisfaction correlation**.

## 2) Pay matters, but less than most people think

The classic result here is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**

Their headline finding: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**.  
In plain English: higher pay helps, but it explains only a **small portion** of why people like or dislike their jobs.

This is one of the more robust findings in the field:
- **pay matters a lot when it is unfair, insufficient, or signals low status**
- but **once compensation is already good**, non-monetary factors often dominate day-to-day wellbeing

For someone already in a very high-paying role, a **“bit higher salary”** is exactly the kind of case where the literature would predict **limited incremental happiness from pay alone**.

## 3) Intrinsic motivation is especially important in creative/research work

This matters for your case because you’re in a research role.

There is a long line of work—especially from **Teresa Amabile** and later meta-analytic work—showing that:

- **intrinsic motivation is especially important for creativity**
- extrinsic incentives can help in some contexts, but for **creative/cognitive work**, being genuinely interested in the work is unusually valuable

Relevant references:
- **Amabile** on intrinsic motivation and creativity
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)** meta-analysis: intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially **quality of performance**

That doesn’t mean “interesting work always beats everything else.”  
But it does mean that for **research/innovation-heavy roles**, interest in the actual work is not just a “nice to have”; it is often central to both **performance** and **sustained satisfaction**.

## 4) Leadership opportunity can matter a lot too—but only if it fits you

This is where your case gets more nuanced.

A role can be **less technically interesting** but still improve wellbeing if it gives you things that are also intrinsically important, such as:
- more **autonomy**
- more **impact**
- more **growth**
- more **leadership**
- stronger **alignment with your ideal self**

This is very consistent with **Self-Determination Theory**:

- **Deci & Ryan**
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** for work settings

Their framework says people do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

A first-team-lead role can increase all three for some people.  
For others, it reduces the enjoyable part of the job and adds stress.

So the literature does **not** say “stay individual contributor” or “take the leadership role.”  
It says: **the key predictor is fit**.

## 5) Person-job fit is one of the best predictors of satisfaction

A very relevant meta-analysis is:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on **person-job fit** and related forms of fit

Broad result:
- when people’s interests, abilities, and values fit the job, they report higher **job satisfaction**, better **commitment**, and lower **intent to quit**

That means the question is not only:
- “Is the work objectively interesting?”

but also:
- “Which role is a better fit for what I actually value now?”

If leading a small team is something you “always wanted,” that may be a serious intrinsic factor, not just a status bump.

## 6) On promotions/management: average benefit, but not always lasting

The promotion literature is more mixed.

What it generally finds:
- promotions often produce a **short-term boost** in job satisfaction
- especially in **pay satisfaction** and **status**
- but the long-run effect is often **smaller** and can **fade**
- management roles also bring more **hours, stress, conflict, and role ambiguity**

So the average finding is not “promotion = happier forever.”  
It is closer to:
- **positive on average**
- **highly heterogeneous**
- strongly dependent on whether the person actually wants managerial work

That heterogeneity is especially relevant for technical people, because “managing” and “doing the interesting technical thing” are often very different jobs.

## 7) On regret specifically: direct evidence is thinner

I don’t know of a strong, clean literature that directly answers:

> “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job rather than taking a higher-paying leadership role?”

That exact question is not well studied.

But there are two related research areas worth noting.

### Affective forecasting / focusing illusion
People are often bad at predicting what will make them happy because they overweight salient, easy-to-compare features.

Relevant work:
- **Schkade & Kahneman (1998)** on the **focusing illusion**
- broader work by **Gilbert, Wilson**, etc. on affective forecasting

Application to jobs:
- salary, title, prestige are easy to compare
- day-to-day texture of the work is harder to simulate
- but the day-to-day texture often matters more for experienced wellbeing

### Hedonic adaptation
People partly adapt to:
- higher income
- titles
- prestige bumps

They adapt less completely to:
- the daily quality of their lived experience

So if a job is less interesting every day, that cost may persist more than the bump from a somewhat higher paycheck or title.

## 8) If I summarize the literature into one evidence-based sentence

A fair summary would be:

> **People generally report higher sustained work wellbeing in jobs that are intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomy-supportive, and a good fit for their values; modest increases in pay usually contribute less to long-run satisfaction than those factors do.**

But there is one major exception:

> **If the new role satisfies a different intrinsic motive that matters deeply to the person—such as leading people, having broader impact, or growing into a new identity—then it may increase wellbeing despite being less interesting in the narrow technical sense.**

## 9) What the literature would predict in a case like yours

Without giving personal advice, the research would generally predict:

- **The slightly higher salary by itself is unlikely to be the decisive wellbeing factor**, especially given your current compensation level.
- **The “much less interesting” part is a real warning sign**, because interest/meaning/autonomy have strong links to job satisfaction.
- **The team-lead opportunity is also a real positive**, if leadership is something you genuinely value rather than something that just sounds good.
- The biggest research-based question is probably not “Meta vs Anthropic,” but:

> **Which role gives you the better day-to-day mix of intrinsic interest, autonomy, meaning, and fit with the person you want to become?**

That is the dimension the literature tends to reward.

---

## Key references

If you want papers to look up, these are good starting points:

1. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   **Journal of Applied Psychology**

2. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   (Often cited for the finding that the relationship is positive but modest.)

3. **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).**  
   *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*

answer:
Yes — there is a pretty solid body of research, mostly from organizational psychology and labor economics.

### Short version

Across studies, **intrinsic job qualities** — especially **interesting work, autonomy, learning, variety, meaning, and fit with your strengths/interests** — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being than pay differences**, especially once income is already high.

A fair summary of the literature is:

- **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
- **Higher pay helps, but the link between pay level and job satisfaction is usually only small to modest.**
- **Promotions/leadership roles often give a short-term boost** in satisfaction/status, but they also tend to bring more stress and the boost often **fades**.
- The biggest moderator is **fit**: if leading people is itself deeply meaningful/interesting to you, then that role may not be “less intrinsic” at all.

So if your choice is really **“more interesting IC work” vs “less interesting work but more money/status”**, research leans toward the former for happiness.  
If the choice is **“interesting research” vs “a kind of leadership I’ve long wanted and would find meaningful”**, the answer becomes less obvious, because leadership itself may satisfy the same intrinsic needs.

---

## What the research says

## 1) “Interesting work” matters a lot

One of the oldest and most replicated findings in work psychology is that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity** (you see a whole piece of work)
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

This is the **Job Characteristics Model**.

### Classic result
**Hackman & Oldham (1976)** found that these characteristics predict internal motivation and satisfaction.

### Meta-analytic result
A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** showed that “motivational” job characteristics such as autonomy, variety, and significance are **substantially associated** with:

- job satisfaction
- engagement/internal motivation
- lower turnover intentions

In plain English: **people tend to be happier in jobs that are inherently engaging and allow real agency**.

That is probably the closest research analogue to “interesting work.”

---

## 2) Meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of work well-being

A related literature looks at **meaningful work** — the sense that your work matters, fits who you are, or serves a worthwhile purpose.

### Meta-analysis
**Allan et al. (2019)** found that meaningful work is **moderately to strongly associated** with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher life satisfaction
- greater commitment
- greater well-being
- lower turnover intentions

The effect sizes here are generally **larger than what you usually see for pay**.

That doesn’t mean meaning is everything. But it does mean that if a role feels much less interesting or less meaningful, that is not a trivial downside — it is one of the biggest predictors of whether people like their jobs.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but usually less than people expect

There’s sometimes a gap between what people predict will make them happier and what actually correlates with sustained job satisfaction.

### Meta-analysis
**Judge et al. (2010)** meta-analyzed the relationship between **pay** and **job satisfaction** and found it is **positive but relatively small** — roughly in the **small-to-modest** range.

That is one of the most important findings for your question.

So:

- If salary goes from “not enough” to “enough,” the effect can be large.
- But among already well-paid knowledge workers, **incremental salary increases usually buy much less satisfaction than people intuitively think**.

This aligns with broader well-being research: income does matter, but **daily experience** and **how you spend your time** matter a lot too, especially once basic and comfort needs are met.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motives generally beat extrinsic motives for well-being

A broad line of research from **Self-Determination Theory** distinguishes between:

- **intrinsic values/goals**: growth, mastery, interest, autonomy, contribution, relationships
- **extrinsic values/goals**: money, status, image

The recurring finding is that prioritizing intrinsic goals is associated with **better well-being**, and extrinsic goals tend to contribute less — sometimes even when achieved.

At work, the basic psychological needs that matter most are:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

When work satisfies those needs, people tend to have:

- higher motivation
- better well-being
- lower burnout

So if your current role gives you a lot of:
- intellectual stimulation,
- competence/mastery,
- autonomy,

that is a very strong well-being package in the research literature.

---

## 5) Promotions and leadership roles: usually a mixed bag

There is research showing that promotions are often associated with:

- higher satisfaction in the short term
- higher pay/status
- more responsibility and stress
- less leisure / more conflict / more pressure

And importantly, some panel studies suggest the happiness effect of promotions often **adapts away** over time.

So a leadership role can absolutely be fulfilling — but empirically it is not a guaranteed improvement in overall well-being.

A useful research-based summary is:

- **Status gains** tend to feel good.
- **People adapt**.
- The day-to-day content of the work matters more in the long run.

That is especially relevant if the new role is “something I always wanted” but the actual work content sounds less interesting. Sometimes people want the *idea* of leading more than the lived daily reality of meetings, coordination, hiring, performance management, and less hands-on technical work.

There is a lot of research on **affective forecasting** showing people are not great at predicting how much future happiness they’ll get from salient achievements like pay or status changes.

---

## 6) Fit may matter more than any single factor

One of the strongest findings in organizational psychology is that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** matter a lot.

If a job matches your interests, strengths, and identity, people tend to report:

- higher satisfaction
- better performance
- lower turnover intention

So the key question is not just:
> “Is interesting work better than money/leadership?”

It is also:
> “Which role better fits the kind of person I am becoming?”

This matters because “leading a small team” could be:
- mostly extrinsic/status-based, or
- deeply intrinsic/meaningful if mentoring, setting direction, and building a team are central to your identity.

Research would predict very different outcomes in those two cases.

---

## 7) On regret specifically

There is **much less direct research** on “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying/leadership one?”

I would be cautious here: I don’t know of a clean, definitive literature answering that exact tradeoff.

But adjacent evidence suggests:

- People often **overestimate** the lasting happiness from salary/status gains.
- People’s long-run well-being is strongly tied to **daily experience**, **meaning**, **autonomy**, and **fit**.
- Pursuing **intrinsic goals** is generally associated with better well-being than pursuing mainly **extrinsic goals**.

So while “regret” per se is not well answered, the literature does **not** support the idea that sacrificing interest/meaning for a somewhat better salary is usually a happiness-maximizing move.

---

# Best evidence-based takeaway for your situation

If I translate the literature into your specific case:

### Research would generally favor staying if:
- Meta work is **substantially more interesting**
- you already earn comfortably high compensation
- the Anthropic role is mostly attractive because of **higher pay/title**
- the leadership component is appealing in principle, but the actual day-to-day work feels less engaging

### Research would make the Anthropic role more attractive if:
- leading a team is itself a major **intrinsic aspiration**
- you’d gain **autonomy, mastery, meaning, and growth**, not just pay
- the role fits your long-term identity better
- the current role is interesting but not developing the capabilities you most care about

In other words, the literature doesn’t say “always choose the interesting job.”  
It says something closer to:

> **Choose the option with the higher long-run intrinsic value and better fit; modest pay increases usually do less for happiness than people expect.**

---

# A few key references

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary. *Journal of Applied Psychology*.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.  
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.  
- **Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L.** Self-Determination Theory work on autonomy, competence, and relatedness.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job/person-organization fit.

---

If you want, I can do one of two useful follow-ups:

1. give you a **more rigorous literature summary with actual effect sizes and caveats**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework strictly based on the research variables** most predictive of later satisfaction.

answer:
Short answer: yes—there is a lot of research suggesting that the “interestingness” of work, usually studied as things like intrinsic motivation, meaningfulness, autonomy, challenge, learning, and person-job fit, is a major predictor of job satisfaction and well-being. In many studies, it matters more than pay once pay is already high enough.

But there is an important nuance for your case: leading a team is not just an extrinsic reward. In the research literature, leadership can itself be an intrinsic good if it gives you more autonomy, impact, mastery, and meaning. So your choice is not simply “interesting work vs money”; it may be “interesting technical content vs leadership/growth/impact.”

## What the research says

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics are strong predictors of satisfaction
A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), which says jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they offer:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

These are all pretty close to what people mean by “interesting work.”

Later **meta-analyses** found that these work-design features are reliably associated with higher job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions. A strong summary is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: large meta-analysis showing motivational job characteristics like autonomy, variety, and significance are robustly related to job satisfaction and motivation.

So on the basic question “do people in more interesting work tend to be happier at work?” the answer is generally **yes**.

### 2) Meaningful work predicts well-being
A related literature studies **meaningful work**. Across many studies, people who experience their work as meaningful report:

- higher job satisfaction
- more engagement
- better general well-being
- lower turnover intentions

A useful reference is:

- **Allan et al.** meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being outcomes

The general result is quite consistent: people do better when their work feels worth doing, not just well-compensated.

### 3) Person-job fit matters a lot
Another strong finding is that people are more satisfied when their work fits their interests, values, and strengths.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: meta-analysis on person-environment fit found that fit is positively related to job satisfaction, commitment, and retention.

So if your current Meta role fits your curiosity and interests much better than the Anthropic role, research would generally predict higher day-to-day satisfaction from staying.

### 4) Pay matters, but less than many people expect
There are two separate questions here:

#### A. Pay and job satisfaction
Research finds that **pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but only modestly**.

- **Judge et al. (2010)**: meta-analysis found the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is real but relatively small.

This is one of the most relevant findings for your situation. It suggests that a “bit higher salary” often does **not** buy a proportionally large increase in job satisfaction.

#### B. Income and overall well-being
On income and happiness more broadly:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income improves life evaluation, while emotional well-being showed a plateau around a certain level in their data.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income on average.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth, & Mellers (2023)** reconciled this somewhat: for most people, well-being rises with income, but with diminishing returns and important subgroup differences.

The practical summary is:

- money does matter
- it matters more when it relieves constraints or insecurity
- at already-high compensation levels, the marginal well-being gain from somewhat more money is usually smaller than people expect

For someone already in a high-paying research role, a moderate salary bump is unlikely, on average, to dominate the effect of the daily quality of the work itself.

### 5) Intrinsic goals tend to beat extrinsic goals for well-being
A broader psychological literature distinguishes **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) from **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image).

- **Kasser & Ryan (1993, 1996)** and related work: prioritizing intrinsic aspirations is generally associated with greater well-being than prioritizing extrinsic ones.

This does **not** mean money and status are bad. It means that when people organize major life choices mainly around extrinsic rewards, they often do worse psychologically than when choices serve intrinsic motives.

That said, “leading a small team” may be intrinsic for you if it represents growth, mastery, and meaningful impact.

### 6) Promotions/leadership are mixed
Research does **not** show that promotions or leadership roles uniformly make people happier.

Why mixed?

Because leadership often brings both:

- more autonomy, impact, and status
- more stress, responsibility, conflict, and time pressure

So whether leadership helps depends a lot on **fit**:

- Do you genuinely want to lead?
- Do you like coaching and people problems?
- Will you have real autonomy?
- Is the team mission meaningful to you?

If yes, leadership can improve satisfaction.
If not, it can become a prestige upgrade with worse day-to-day experience.

## What about regret?
This is the weakest part of the evidence base for your exact question. I do **not** know of a strong body of research directly comparing:

- people who stayed in a more interesting role
- versus people who took a somewhat better-paid, less interesting leadership role

That said, two related findings are relevant:

### A. Long-run regrets often concern missed meaningful opportunities
In classic regret research:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** found that in the long run, people more often regret **inactions** than actions.

That result is broad, not career-specific, but it suggests people often regret not pursuing something they felt drawn to.

### B. People often adapt to raises/status gains
A lot of well-being research suggests people adapt relatively quickly to improvements in income or status. Day-to-day work content, by contrast, keeps affecting you repeatedly because you live inside it every week.

So if a job is less interesting every day, that cost can be persistent, while the emotional benefit of “higher salary” or “new title” may fade faster.

## The most research-grounded way to frame your decision
Your decision is not best described as:

- **interesting work** vs **higher salary**

It is more accurately:

- **interesting research content**
vs
- **leadership/autonomy/impact + somewhat higher salary + less interesting content**

That matters because the first and second bundles both contain intrinsic goods.

From the literature, the biggest predictors of satisfaction are likely to be:

1. **daily task enjoyment / intellectual interest**
2. **autonomy**
3. **meaning / significance**
4. **fit with your values and strengths**
5. **growth/mastery**
6. **quality of colleagues**
7. **pay**, but with smaller marginal returns once already high

So if “leading a small team” is something you’ve “always wanted,” research would not treat that as a trivial perk. It may be a major intrinsic benefit that could offset a loss in topic-interest.

## Bottom line from the research
If I strip away personal opinion and stay close to the evidence:

- **Interesting, meaningful, well-fitting work is a strong and consistent predictor of job satisfaction and well-being.**
- **Higher pay has a positive but usually smaller effect on job satisfaction, especially at already high income levels.**
- **Leadership/promotion is not reliably happiness-improving on its own; it helps mainly when it fits the person and increases autonomy, growth, and meaning.**
- **Direct evidence on “regret” for this exact type of career tradeoff is limited.**
- **A somewhat higher salary alone would not usually be expected to compensate for much less interesting work.**
- **But a move into leadership could still improve your well-being if leading people is itself a deeply valued intrinsic goal for you.**

If you want, I can give you a **short reading list of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses** on this, or turn the research into a **decision checklist** using variables that actually predict satisfaction.

answer:
Yes—there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not usually framed as “interesting job vs slightly higher salary plus management.” The strongest evidence comes from occupational psychology, motivation research, and happiness economics.

## Short version

The research fairly consistently suggests:

1. **The nature of the work itself**—especially **interesting/challenging work, autonomy, skill use, learning, and meaning**—is a **strong predictor of job satisfaction and well-being**.
2. **Pay matters**, but once compensation is already high enough that basic financial stress is low, **additional pay tends to have a smaller effect on job satisfaction than people often expect**.
3. **Fit matters a lot**: if leading a team is something you genuinely value, that can be a major positive because leadership can increase **autonomy, impact, identity, and growth**.
4. There is **less direct research on “regret”** specifically. Most studies measure job satisfaction, engagement, burnout, commitment, and turnover intentions. On those measures, **intrinsic job qualities usually beat modest extrinsic gains**.

So if your question is: **“Do people who stay in more interesting work tend to be happier?”**  
The research-based answer is broadly **yes, on average**, especially when “interesting” means high in **autonomy, challenge, meaning, and skill variety**.

---

## The main research findings

### 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A classic and still influential framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). It says that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- **Skill variety**
- **Task identity**
- **Task significance**
- **Autonomy**
- **Feedback**

These are basically the scientific versions of “interesting,” “meaningful,” and “I get to do real work that uses my abilities.”

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large literature on work design.
- They found that characteristics like **autonomy, task significance, skill variety, and feedback** were **consistently and substantially associated** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal motivation
  - engagement
  - lower strain/burnout-related outcomes

In plain English: **the content and structure of the work matter a lot**.

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work by **Bryan Allan and colleagues** found that meaningful work is positively associated with:

- **job satisfaction**
- **work engagement**
- **life meaning**
- **organizational commitment**

and negatively associated with:

- **turnover intentions**
- **burnout / psychological strain**

This matters for your case because “interesting” work is often not just cognitively engaging—it also feels **personally meaningful**.

### 3. Pay has a positive effect, but it is usually smaller than intrinsic job quality

There is a meta-analysis specifically on **pay and job satisfaction**:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

That is one of the most relevant findings for your question.

This doesn’t mean salary is irrelevant. It means:

- money helps,
- but **the direct link between salary and liking your job is not that large**,
- especially relative to intrinsic qualities like autonomy, interesting tasks, and fit.

This is also consistent with broader happiness research:

- **Income improves well-being**, especially when it reduces stress and expands real freedom.
- But people also **adapt** to higher income, and its effect is often partly relative/comparative rather than absolute.

Relevant broader papers:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: income strongly relates to life evaluation; experienced well-being showed diminishing returns.
- **Killingsworth (2021)**: found well-being continues to rise with income on average, though this is about life happiness overall, not job satisfaction specifically.

For your decision, the key point is: **“a bit higher salary” usually does not buy nearly as much job satisfaction as people imagine, if both options are already highly paid.**

### 4. Autonomy is especially important

Across many studies, **autonomy** is one of the strongest job predictors of:

- satisfaction
- motivation
- lower burnout
- better well-being

This shows up in both work-design research and **Self-Determination Theory**.

Self-Determination Theory says people function best when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Reviews and meta-analytic evidence in this area consistently find that **need satisfaction at work** predicts:
- better well-being,
- stronger motivation,
- better performance,
- less exhaustion.

So a role that gives you more ownership, more discretion, or more ability to shape a team may matter a lot—even if the subject matter is somewhat less intellectually interesting.

### 5. Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction

This may be especially relevant because you said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.”

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on person-environment fit

found that **person-job fit** and related fit constructs are strongly associated with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **organizational commitment**
- **lower intent to quit**
- better performance-related outcomes

This is important because it suggests the question is not just:

- “Which job is more interesting?”

but also:

- “Which job fits what I most want at this stage of my life?”

If you deeply want to lead, then a management role may satisfy a form of intrinsic motivation—not just provide status.

---

## What the literature says about “regret”

This part is weaker.

There is **not much direct research** on:
- “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying one?”
- “Do people regret staying individual-contributor rather than becoming a manager?”

Researchers usually study:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- burnout
- turnover intentions
- commitment
- mental well-being

rather than explicit long-term regret.

Still, two findings are relevant:

### A. People often mispredict what will matter most
Research on **affective forecasting** suggests people are not great at predicting what will make them happy long-term. They often overestimate the lasting impact of:
- money,
- prestige,
- one-time gains,

and underestimate the impact of:
- daily experience,
- fit,
- ongoing meaning,
- quality of relationships.

That doesn’t prove you’d regret one choice or the other, but it does imply that the **day-to-day texture of the work** may matter more than the headline difference.

### B. Extrinsic goals tend to be less associated with well-being than intrinsic goals
In the broader motivation literature, especially work influenced by **Kasser & Ryan**, prioritizing **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image) over **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) is generally associated with **lower well-being**.

Important nuance:  
In your case, **leading a team may be intrinsic**, not extrinsic, if it represents growth, impact, and identity rather than just title/status.

---

## So what seems most supported by evidence?

If I strip this down to the strongest research-backed conclusions:

### Most supported:
- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction.**
- **Pay matters, but its impact on job satisfaction is modest compared with intrinsic job features.**
- **Fit with your motives and values matters a lot.**
- **A modest pay bump alone usually does not compensate for less interesting work, if both jobs already pay very well.**

### Also supported:
- **Leadership can increase satisfaction if it fulfills intrinsic motives** like growth, autonomy, and impact.
- But management also often brings **more demands, coordination burden, stress, and less hands-on technical work**, so its benefit is more person-dependent.

### Not strongly established:
- Direct evidence about **career regret** in your exact tradeoff.
- A universal answer to “IC in fascinating work vs manager in less fascinating work,” because this depends heavily on **what you value most right now**.

---

## The most relevant papers / literatures to look up

If you want to read the strongest sources, I’d start with these:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   Meta-analysis of work design/job characteristics.

2. **Judge et al. (2010)**  
   Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   Meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit.

4. **Allan et al.**  
   Meta-analytic work on meaningful work and its outcomes.

5. **Gagné & Deci (2005)** and later Self-Determination Theory reviews/meta-analyses  
   On autonomy, competence, relatedness, and work motivation.

6. **Clark, Frijters, & Shields (2008)**  
   Review on income, happiness, adaptation, and social comparison.

7. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and **Killingsworth (2021)**  
   On income and well-being more broadly.

---

## Research-based summary in one sentence

If both jobs already clear the “very well compensated” threshold, the literature would generally predict that **long-run satisfaction depends more on the work being engaging, meaningful, and fitting your motives than on a modest salary increase**—but **wanting to lead a team** is itself a potentially major intrinsic factor, not just a perk.

If you want, I can next give you a **more technical summary with actual effect sizes / correlation magnitudes** from the main meta-analyses.

answer:
Yes. There’s a fairly strong research literature suggesting that the **intrinsic quality of the work**—how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-promoting it is—matters a lot for job satisfaction and wellbeing, often **more than incremental pay differences**, especially once someone is already well-paid.

The main caveat is that the evidence on **“regret” after this exact tradeoff** is much thinner than the evidence on **happiness/satisfaction**.

## Short answer

What the literature most consistently says is:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work predicts higher job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is positive and fairly modest.**
3. **At high income levels, extra money tends to have smaller wellbeing returns than people expect.**
4. **There’s less direct evidence on regret**, but broader regret research suggests people often regret **not pursuing identity-relevant aspirations** more than they regret taking a shot and learning from it.

So if the choice is:

- **more interesting work**
vs
- **slightly more pay + leadership opportunity, but less interesting work**

then the literature would generally predict that the **more interesting work** is better for **day-to-day happiness and job satisfaction**, while the **leadership opportunity** is the part most likely to matter for possible **long-run regret**, if leading people is a deep aspiration for you.

---

## What research says

### 1) Intrinsic job characteristics are strong predictors of satisfaction

A classic line of research in organizational psychology shows that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/master**

This is the core of the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later **meta-analyses** found these features are reliably associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal.

A good meta-analytic reference is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

Their overall conclusion: how a job is designed matters a lot, and **motivational characteristics** like autonomy, variety, and significance are strongly linked to positive outcomes.

For your question, “interesting work” maps pretty well onto these constructs.

---

### 2) Meaningful work is strongly associated with wellbeing

A related literature looks at **meaningful work**. People who see their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- greater engagement
- better general wellbeing
- lower turnover intention

A useful reference:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019/2020)**, *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*

The exact framing varies across papers, but the overall result is very consistent: **meaningfulness is not a minor perk; it is a substantial predictor of wellbeing at work.**

If your current work is “very interesting” in a way that feels intellectually alive or meaningful, the literature would treat that as psychologically important, not as a luxury.

---

### 3) Pay matters, but less than many people expect

There is strong evidence that **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction**, but the effect is **modest**, not dominant.

One of the most-cited meta-analyses is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*

A commonly cited takeaway from that paper is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is only around **0.15**—real, but not huge.

That means:
- money is not irrelevant;
- but it is usually **far from the strongest driver** of satisfaction.

This is especially relevant in your case because you’re not choosing between “financial insecurity” and “comfort.” You’re comparing two already elite jobs with a **somewhat higher salary** on one side.

---

### 4) Once basic needs and comfort are covered, daily experience matters a lot

There’s also broader wellbeing research showing that income improves wellbeing, but people often **overweight income/status differences** when imagining how happy they’ll be.

Relevant papers include:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** on income and wellbeing
- **Killingsworth (2021)**, which complicated the old “plateau” view but still supports the idea that money helps, not that it dominates all else
- **Schkade & Kahneman (1998)** on the **focusing illusion**: people exaggerate the importance of salient differences when predicting happiness

The practical implication is not “money doesn’t matter.” It’s:

> When making choices, people often overweight the visible, socially legible variables—salary, title, prestige—and underweight the quality of daily experience.

For work decisions, that usually means underweighting:
- “Will I be absorbed by the work?”
- “Will I feel energized?”
- “Will I want to do this every day?”

---

### 5) Autonomy, competence, and relatedness matter a lot

**Self-Determination Theory** is one of the most robust frameworks here. It argues that wellbeing is higher when work supports three psychological needs:

- **autonomy** — having meaningful choice/control
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

In work contexts, jobs that satisfy these needs tend to produce more:
- intrinsic motivation
- persistence
- satisfaction
- psychological wellbeing

A useful review:

- **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)**, *Self-Determination Theory in Work Organizations: The State of a Science.*

This matters because your two options may differ on these dimensions in a non-obvious way:

- The Anthropic role may increase **autonomy/status/relatedness** through leadership.
- Your current Meta role may better support **competence/intrinsic motivation** if the work itself is much more interesting.

Research would say both sides matter. But if the new role is “less interesting” in a deep sense, that likely means a hit to intrinsic motivation and competence satisfaction.

---

### 6) Person-job fit predicts satisfaction

Another major line of evidence is **person-environment fit**, especially **person-job fit** and **person-vocation fit**.

If your work fits your interests, strengths, and preferred mode of working, you’re more likely to experience:
- satisfaction
- persistence
- lower burnout/turnover

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

This doesn’t mean “follow your passion” in a simplistic way. It means mismatch has measurable psychological costs.

So if you viscerally feel “this work is much less interesting to me,” that’s not soft or irrational; it’s exactly the kind of fit signal the literature treats as important.

---

## What about regret?

This is where the evidence is less direct.

I’m not aware of strong research specifically showing that people later regret choosing the more interesting job over a somewhat better-paid leadership job, or vice versa. That exact choice is hard to study cleanly.

But there are two relevant literatures:

### A) People often regret inaction more than action in the long run
A classic result:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** found that in the long run, people tend to regret **inactions** more than **actions**.

That does **not** mean “you should take the Anthropic job.”
It means something narrower:

> If “leading a small team” is a long-standing aspiration that feels central to your identity, passing on it may be a source of future regret even if it would not maximize short-run job enjoyment.

This is probably the most relevant research angle on your “I always wanted to do that” comment.

### B) Regret is often strongest around the “ideal self”
Related work on regret and counterfactuals suggests people are especially prone to regret choices that feel like failures to pursue their **ideal self** or core aspirations.

So there are two different predictions:

- If your central value is **interesting research**, you may regret leaving it.
- If your central aspiration is **becoming a leader**, you may regret not trying.

The literature does not give one universal answer because regret depends a lot on identity and aspirations, not just average happiness effects.

---

## So, do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?

**On average: yes.**  
At least in the sense of:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- lower burnout/turnover intention
- better day-to-day work experience

That’s one of the clearest findings in work psychology.

If the alternative is only **a bit more money**, the research generally favors **interesting, meaningful, well-fit work** as the stronger predictor of happiness.

---

## Do they regret it?

The evidence is much less direct.

What I’d say, sticking closely to the research, is:

- There is **strong evidence** that more interesting/meaningful work is better for satisfaction.
- There is **weak direct evidence** on later regret from this exact tradeoff.
- The best-supported regret-related finding is that people often regret **not pursuing important aspirations**.

So in your case, the question is not only:
- “Which job is more interesting?”

but also:
- “Is leading a team a core aspiration, or just an attractive badge?”

Research would predict very different long-run feelings depending on that answer.

---

## If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

For someone already in a high-paying elite role, **the marginal boost from somewhat higher pay is usually smaller than the wellbeing value of doing work that is genuinely interesting, meaningful, and well-matched to them**—unless the alternative role fulfills a major identity-level aspiration, in which case **regret from not trying it** becomes a serious factor.

---

## Useful references to look up

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — meta-analysis on fit at work
- **Deci, E. L., Olafsen, A. H., & Ryan, R. M. (2017)** — Self-Determination Theory in work organizations
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L.** — meta-analysis on meaningful work
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010)** — income and wellbeing
- **Schkade, D. A., & Kahneman, D. (1998)** — focusing illusion
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. (1995)** — regrets of action vs inaction

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision memo grounded in the literature**—for example, a one-page framework with weighted factors like:
- intrinsic interest
- leadership aspiration
- autonomy
- future option value
- burnout risk
- regret risk

without adding personal opinions.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Meta doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Anthropic - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

There must be some research on how important is doing interesting work comparing to other factors. Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier? Or do they regret that? Can you recall any results on that? I'm mostly interested in research results, not personal opinions.
